entries.text: Open, Save, and Save As for .rms files.
Not implemented yet.
App settings, including how these help popups behave.
Not implemented yet.
The name of the currently open map, taken from its filename. Shows "Untitled Map" until you open or save a file.
When this map was last saved during this session. Shows a dash until you save at least once.
Shows your script as editable command blocks instead of raw code — good for building without memorizing syntax.
The full code editor, with RMS syntax highlighting and hover docs.
A pane for built-in and (later) community-made tools that operate on your script. Arrives in Phase 5.
Total resources across the whole map, summed from every create_object placement the parser can see. Shown as a min-max range when if/start_random blocks make the exact count generation-dependent.

Resources a single player gets from set_place_for_every_player placements — per player, not multiplied by player count (see Total for the map-wide sum across every player). v1 doesn't yet correlate placement with a specific player's land.
Resources placed without set_place_for_every_player — everything not counted as Player. v1 doesn't yet check whether these sit inside or outside player territory.
Errors and warnings found by the parser in the current file, re-checked live as you type on the Code tab. Click a squiggle in the editor to see the full message.
Opens Generation Settings — map size and player count, used to compute the resource totals shown here.
How many players this map is generated for. Scales resources placed with set_place_for_every_player in the totals above.
The map size this script targets. Not yet used by the resource totals — will feed the approximate preview and consistency checker in a later phase.
Controls when these help popups appear: always on hover, only while holding ALT, or off entirely.
Global constants and directives (#define/#const/#include) that appear before the first section — the natural home for defining a map-wide constant.
A section name the parser doesn't recognize (likely a typo in a <SECTION_HEADER>). Its contents still render below so you can see and fix it.
Where starting positions and team placement are configured.
Commands that shape each player's land mass.
Commands that add hills and elevation.
Commands that place cliffs.
Commands that paint additional terrain types onto the map.
Commands that connect player areas (roads, water paths) and their terrain along the way.
Commands that place resources, forests, and other objects.
Opens a searchable list of commands for this section to insert. Filtered to this section by default — toggle "show all sections" to search everything.
Search commands from every section, not just this tab's. RMS doesn't enforce section placement, so this is a convenience filter, not a restriction.
Type to filter the command list by name or description. Escape closes the picker.

Click a command to insert it. The chip shows whether this command's reference data has been verified against the official docs — "unverified" entries may have incomplete or unconfirmed argument details, but are still safe to use.
Click +/− to expand this command and see or edit its arguments and attributes.
Expands or collapses this command's arguments and attributes.
Removes this command (and its block) from the script. Undo with Ctrl+Z.
A positional argument this command takes. Commits when you click away or press Enter; Escape reverts.
This argument's name — hover shows what it's for, sourced from the reference docs (falls back to type/range/default when no description is written yet).
This attribute's current value, taken directly from the code. Commits when you click away or press Enter; Escape reverts to the current value.
This attribute's name — hover shows what it does, sourced from the reference docs.
A checkbox attribute — checked means the flag is present in the code, unchecked means it's absent. Toggling adds or removes the flag immediately.
This attribute appears more than once in the code, so every instance is listed and edited independently — none of them are merged or overwritten.
Not present in the code yet. This faint row is a placeholder, not real content — clicking + inserts the attribute into the code.
Removes this attribute (or this one instance of it, if it appears more than once) from the code. Undo with Ctrl+Z.
Adds another instance of this repeatable attribute.
A recognized attribute that isn't in this command's usual list, but is still valid and editable.
An if/elseif/else block. Each branch's contents render below it and are fully editable.
The label this branch runs under (a game mode, a #define you made, etc). Commits on blur/Enter; free text is always accepted.
Adds a command inside this branch.
Add another elseif or an else branch (only one else is allowed).
Removes this whole if/elseif/else block, including all its branches.
Removes just this branch. Disabled when it's the only branch left — delete the whole conditional instead.
A start_random block — each percent_chance branch has a weight and its own contents; exactly one branch runs when the map generates.
This branch's percent_chance weight. Commits on blur/Enter.
Adds a command inside this branch.
Adds another percent_chance branch.
Removes this whole start_random/end_random block, including all its branches.
Removes just this branch. Disabled when it's the only branch left — delete the whole random block instead.
A preprocessor directive like #define or #const. Commits on blur/Enter; a quoted filename argument (#include_drs/#includeXS) keeps its quotes automatically.
This directive is accepted by the game but does nothing in Definitive Edition — safe to leave, but it won't have any effect.
Removes this directive line from the script.
A problem somewhere in this card, coloured by its worst severity. Click to jump straight to it.
A comment from your script, shown here for reference. Comments aren't editable in Breakdown yet — switch to the Code tab to change them.

This code couldn't be shown as an editable block — usually because it's unusual/exotic RMS the parser intentionally leaves alone rather than risk misrepresenting it. The exact text from your file is shown below, unchanged.
Applies the suggested spelling in place — the region usually turns into a normal editable card once it re-parses.
This command name isn't recognized, but it's close to a known one — applies the suggested spelling in place. The card usually turns into a fully recognized command once it re-parses.
Jumps to this exact spot on the Code tab so you can edit it directly. Not yet wired up.
An attribute written outside any command's { } block — it has no effect where it sits. Move it inside the intended command's block.
A parser diagnostic (error/warning/info) applies somewhere in this card — the same ones shown as squiggles on the Code tab.
Switches the map preview between the current in-progress view and the final generated result. Preview logic arrives in Phase 4.
Switches the reference table below between Terrain constants, Object constants, and Commands.
A read-only lookup of the constants/commands available in RMS, sourced from this app's reference data. Some columns are placeholders pending later data-extraction work.
Cancels the action. Age of RMS stays as it is and nothing is saved or discarded.
Cancels the action, exactly like the Cancel button. Pressing Esc or clicking outside the dialog does the same.
Saves this map first, then continues with what you were doing (closing, or opening another map). If the map has never been saved, you'll be asked where to put it.
Continues WITHOUT saving — every change made since the last save is permanently discarded.
